title: Aloo gobi
subtitle: Potato and cauliflower, dry-fried with cumin, turmeric and ginger.
excerpt: Cubes of potato and florets of cauliflower steam-fried in a covered pan with onion, ginger, garlic and a short list of warming spices. No sauce, no cream — the dish dries down to glossy yellow cubes that hold their shape. Forty-five minutes start to finish; works as a weeknight main with rice or a side beside a wetter curry.
sourceNotes:
Colonel A. R. Kenney-Herbert ('Wyvern'), Culinary Jottings for Madras (1878) — Project Gutenberg public-domain source. Wyvern's writing on Anglo-Indian vegetable cookery established the dry-fried ("bhuna") method that aloo gobi sits within: aromatics in hot fat, the vegetable cooked covered until tender, finished uncovered to drive off moisture.

The dish as it now reads in British-Indian restaurants is a later codification, shaped by Punjabi and Bangladeshi kitchens in the post-war curry-house trade. Proportions and method here follow the dry-fry tradition rather than a single citable text; the spice ratios are restaurant-tradition standards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes for two months in a sealed container. The cauliflower softens a little on defrosting; reheat covered in a low oven (140°C) with a splash of water to bring it back. Better fresh, but the freezer version is still a useful weeknight standby.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly in a wide pan; the vegetables need room to steam-fry without crowding. For a triple batch, use two pans rather than piling one too deep.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The spice mix can be ground and the aromatics chopped an hour ahead. The finished dish reheats well; the flavours actually deepen overnight in the fridge.

recipeTools.notes: Wide 28 cm pan with a lid. The lid is essential — the cauliflower steams covered, then dries uncovered.
For grating ginger and garlic into a paste.

glossaryTerms.definition: A short, intense bloom of whole spices in hot fat at the start of a dish (or sometimes spooned over at the end). The spices crackle and release their fat-soluble aromatics within seconds. In aloo gobi the tarka is at the start: cumin seeds in hot oil, lid off, listened for the pop, before the onion goes in.

A method as much as a dish: dry-frying spiced aromatics until the oil separates from the paste, then cooking the main ingredient with little or no added liquid. Bhuna curries are thicker and more concentrated than gravy curries. The visual cue is the oil pulling free of the spice base — a sign the spices have cooked out and lost their raw edge.

content.text: Aloo gobi is the dry-fried potato and cauliflower curry of the British-Indian restaurant canon. No tomato sauce, no cream — the vegetables steam-fry in a covered pan with onion, ginger, garlic, and a short list of warming spices, then finish uncovered until the moisture cooks off and the cubes turn glossy yellow. Forty-five minutes start to finish, mostly hands-off after the chopping.
The method matters more than the spice list. Cumin seeds go into hot oil first, a short
tarka

before the onion joins. The onion browns properly; the ginger and garlic paste cooks out for a minute; the ground spices bloom in the fat until the smell turns toasty. Only then do the potato and cauliflower go in. The pan covers, the vegetables soften in their own steam, the lid comes off, and the last few minutes drive the moisture out.

Two working notes. First, the potato cubes want to be smaller than the cauliflower florets, about 2 cm against 3 cm, so they cook at the same rate. Second, the lid is not optional. The pan with the lid on acts as a steamer; without it, the vegetables dry out before they soften and the dish reads as half-cooked.

attrs.title: Why no tomato
attrs.body: Aloo gobi belongs to the dry-fried bhuna family, not the gravy-curry family. Adding tomato turns it into a different dish (closer to aloo gobi masala in some restaurant menus). The dish reads at its best when the spice and aromatics carry the flavour and the vegetables provide the substance. If you want a wetter curry, the troubleshooting section below covers it; for the dish as written, leave the tomato out.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: peeled, cut into 2 cm cubes; Maris Piper or any waxy variety
broken into 3 cm florets; ~700 g once trimmed
deseeded and finely chopped; leave seeds in for more heat
juice of half a lemon, to finish
a small handful, chopped, to finish

content.text: A wide sauté pan with a tight-fitting lid is the only specialist kit. A microplane makes the ginger and garlic paste in under a minute; a small grater or a sharp knife and a flat blade works too.
Method
Prep the vegetables and the aromatics

Peel and cut {{potato}} of potato into 2 cm cubes; sit them in a bowl of cold water while you prep the rest. Break {{cauliflower}} cauliflower into florets about 3 cm across; smaller is fine, larger will undercook. Grate {{ginger-root}} of ginger and {{garlic}} of garlic into a small heap. Finely chop {{onion}} onion and {{chilli-green}} green chilli.
Start the tarka

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat. Once it shimmers, add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds. They should sizzle and start popping within five seconds. Let them go for fifteen seconds — the smell should turn toasty, not sharp.
Cook the onion

Tip in the chopped onion and chilli with a pinch of salt. Cook for eight minutes, stirring every couple of minutes, until the onion is soft and starting to turn golden at the edges. Lower the heat if the cumin seeds threaten to burn.
Bloom the spices

Stir in the grated ginger and garlic; cook for a minute until fragrant. Add {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, and {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder. Stir for thirty seconds: the spices should look glossy and the oil should start pulling away at the edges of the paste — the
bhuna
visual cue. Splash in a tablespoon of water if the paste sticks.
Add the potato, then the cauliflower

Drain the potato cubes and tip them into the pan along with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir to coat every cube in the spice paste. Add the cauliflower florets and stir gently — the florets are more fragile than the potato. Splash in 50 ml of water, clap the lid on, and turn the heat down to medium-low.
Cover and steam-fry

Cook covered for fifteen minutes, lifting the lid to give it a gentle stir every five minutes. The potato should be just tender to a knife tip and the cauliflower should give to gentle pressure but still hold its shape. If the pan looks dry before then, splash in another two tablespoons of water.
Dry the dish down

Take the lid off and turn the heat back up to medium. Cook uncovered for five minutes more, stirring carefully so the cauliflower doesn't break up, until the pan looks dry and glossy rather than wet. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Pull off the heat. Squeeze over the juice of half a lemon, scatter with chopped fresh coriander, and serve with basmati rice or naan.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Aloo gobi goes wrong at the same three points: undercooked potato, mushy cauliflower, and a flat-tasting dish from spices that didn't bloom.

items.symptom: Potato still hard when the cauliflower is done.
items.cause: Cubes were too large, or went into the pan after the cauliflower.
items.fix: Potato needs to be 2 cm or smaller and go in first; the cauliflower should join thirty seconds later. If the potato is still hard at the lid-off stage, splash in a little water and re-cover for another five minutes before drying the pan back down.

items.symptom: Cauliflower has broken down to mush.
items.cause: Stirred too often, or the heat was too high under the lid.
items.fix: Stir gently and only every five minutes during the covered stage. Medium-low under the lid; the cauliflower wants to steam, not boil. Larger florets are also more forgiving than small.

items.symptom: Dish tastes flat, with a raw-spice edge.
items.fix: The thirty-second cook of the ground spices in the hot fat is the step that cooks out the raw edge. The visual cue is the oil starting to pull away from the spice paste. Splash in water if it sticks, but cook the spices through.

items.symptom: Whole pan tastes too salty or too plain.
items.cause: Salt added all in one go or not at all.
items.fix: Add the teaspoon of salt with the potato, then taste-correct at the end with a pinch more if it needs it. Salting the spice paste before the vegetables go in usually over-seasons; salting the vegetables once they're coated is the safer route.

items.symptom: Dish too dry; pan stuck and the cauliflower didn't soften.
items.cause: Not enough liquid under the lid, or the heat was too high.
items.fix: Two splashes of water (50 ml at the start, another 30 ml mid-cook if needed) and the lid on tight produces enough steam for the vegetables to soften without the pan drying out. Turn the heat down if you hear the pan crackling rather than gently bubbling.

content.text: Variations

For a wetter version closer to the restaurant-menu "aloo gobi masala", add a tin of chopped tomatoes after the spice bloom and simmer for ten minutes before the vegetables join — the dish becomes a thick gravy rather than dry-fry. Peas go in well with the cauliflower; a handful at the same point adds colour and sweetness. Adding diced paneer in the last five minutes makes it a fuller dinner. For a sharper finish, scatter in chopped green chilli with the coriander rather than cooking it through.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The dish reheats well in a low oven covered with foil and a splash of water; the flavours deepen overnight in the fridge and many British home cooks prefer it the next day. It freezes for two months in a sealed container, though the cauliflower softens slightly on defrosting. Cold leftovers fold into a vegetable wrap or sit on top of a baked potato with a spoon of yoghurt.
Where this dish lives

Aloo gobi sits in the dry-fried vegetable tradition of Punjabi home cooking, carried into British kitchens via the post-war curry-house trade. The Anglo-Indian writing of Colonel Kenney-Herbert in 1878 already documented the bhuna method: aromatics in hot oil, vegetable cooked covered, finished uncovered to concentrate. The dish itself, under that name, is later — a staple of London-Punjabi and Bradford-Bangladeshi kitchens by the 1970s and on most British restaurant menus by the 1980s.

At home it earns its place as a weekday side beside a wetter curry like lamb rogan josh or chicken bhuna, where its dry texture contrasts the gravy. It also stands on its own as a vegetarian dinner with rice and a bowl of yoghurt, which is how Punjabi families have eaten it for generations. The spice list is short enough to keep in the cupboard year-round; the dish is the kind of midweek standard that grows on a cook the more often they make it.
